title: Leadership Across Generations: Navigating AI Adoption in Family Businesses
description:
1. Project Aim
This project examines how leadership influences the speed and success of AI adoption in organisations, with a focus on family businesses navigating generational leadership transitions. Leadership plays a pivotal role in shaping organisational culture, responding to external pressures, and determining technological readiness. In family enterprises, succession challenges and differing generational perspectives on innovation can either accelerate or hinder AI adoption. This study aims to identify leadership strategies that foster successful AI integration while maintaining business continuity.
2. Project Gap
Existing research on AI adoption primarily focuses on technical, individual, or industry-specific factors, overlooking the complex interplay of leadership, organisational structure, and external pressures. While leadership is recognised as crucial in digital transformation, its specific role—particularly in family businesses where intergenerational shifts impact decision-making—remains underexplored. This project addresses this gap by integrating leadership theories with technology adoption frameworks to develop a holistic model that accounts for organisational, technological, and generational dynamics.
3. Methodology
An explanatory sequential mixed-methods approach will be employed. First, a quantitative survey will assess AI adoption speed, project success, leadership styles, and contextual factors, using statistical modelling to test mediation and moderation effects. Then, qualitative multiple case studies will provide deeper insights into how leadership practices in family businesses shape AI adoption. This phase will explore generational differences in AI perceptions, implementation challenges, and leadership strategies for aligning innovation with long-term sustainability.
team_leader: Aemen Nasir, Nhu Do

title: Exploring the Potential of Game-based Learning in Higher Education and Corporate Environments
description:
In today’s educational landscape, traditional teaching methods often fail to engage learners effectively, necessitating new strategies for collaboration. Despite the rising use of gamification, its effectiveness in higher education and corporate training is under-researched, creating valuable opportunities for study.

Game-based learning promotes hands-on engagement and active participation through tools like Lego® Serious Play® (LSP®), card games, and digital games, fostering creativity and problem-solving. This proposal invites PhD candidates to explore how these methodologies enhance student engagement and corporate training experiences. Potential research topics include improving knowledge retention, critical thinking, and creativity or facilitating team building within organizations.

The supervision team includes certified LSP® facilitators to support students in mastering relevant skills. Internships with partner organizations will allow candidates to implement gamification strategies, applying theoretical knowledge and gaining practical experience that benefits both their research and the organizations.

Ideal candidates should have backgrounds in education, organizational development, or psychology, and possess a passion for innovative learning or corporate training methodologies. We invite forward-thinking researchers to apply for this opportunity to explore game-based learning's potential while gaining valuable hands-on experience through internships.
team_leader: Trang Le, E.Y.T. Chew
for_codes: 350599 Human resources and industrial relations not elsewhere classified

title: From Perceptions to Practices: Understanding Meaningful Work in Human Resource Management amidst Digital Transformation
description:
As organizations rapidly adopt digital technologies, the concept of meaningful work is evolving, presenting new challenges and opportunities for Human Resource Management (HRM). Employees increasingly seek roles that offer not just compensation but also a sense of purpose. This proposal aims to recruit HDR candidates to explore the intricacies of meaningful work within the HR context, emphasizing how digital transformation impacts employee engagement, recruitment, retention, and organizational culture in ways that align with individuals' desires for fulfillment.

The modern workplace, influenced by technological advancements, is changing the traditional employer-employee relationship. Remote work and AI-driven HR practices alter how employees connect with their roles. Understanding how these changes influence perceptions of meaningful work is crucial for HR professionals aiming to develop strategies that enhance employee satisfaction and retention.

PhD candidates will investigate pressing questions at the intersection of meaningful work and HRM within the digital landscape. They may explore topics such as job design for enhanced engagement, effective recruitment strategies that communicate organizational culture, and the role of digital platforms in fostering diverse perspectives on meaningful work.

Ideal candidates will have a background in HRM, organizational behavior, or a related field, along with a strong interest in advancing both quantitative and qualitative research skills. We encourage aspiring researchers to apply for this transformative opportunity to study the dynamics of meaningful work in the digital era, contributing valuable insights to both academic literature and organizational practices.
team_leader: Thao Luong, Trang Le
for_codes: 350507 Workplace wellbeing and quality of working life

title: Green Transformational Leadership and Employee Sustainability Behaviours as Drivers of Green Innovation: Evidence from Vietnamese Manufacturing Firms
description:
Manufacturing firms in Vietnam are facing increasing pressure to adopt sustainable business practices due to rising environmental concerns, global supply chain requirements, environmental, social, and governance (ESG) expectations, and stricter regulatory standards. As Vietnam continues to integrate into international production networks, sustainability and green innovation have become important sources of competitiveness, operational efficiency, and long-term resilience for manufacturing companies. However, much of the literature remains fragmented, with studies often focusing either on organisational-level sustainability strategies or on isolated employee behavioural factors. Limited attention has been paid to understanding how leadership and sustainability orientation shape employee-level mechanisms that subsequently contribute to green innovation within organisations.

Recent studies suggest that leadership and CSR practices encourages employee green creativity, knowledge sharing, pro-environmental behaviour, and environmental commitment, all of which may contribute to innovation outcomes. Nevertheless, the conditions under which these behavioural mechanisms translate into successful green innovation remain underexplored. Furthermore, the growing emphasis on ESG practices and sustainability governance has created new challenges for organisations attempting to align strategic sustainability objectives with employee engagement and innovation performance. However, the mechanisms through which sustainability-oriented leadership influences green innovation in Vietnamese manufacturing firms remain underexplored. In particular, many companies continue to face challenges in translating sustainability goals into employee engagement and operational innovation.

Therefore, this project aim to examine how green transformational leadership and sustainability-oriented organisational practices influence employee-level sustainability behaviours and green innovation within Vietnamese manufacturing firms. The study may also explore the moderating role of organisational climate or governance structures in strengthening these relationships. The findings are expected to provide practical insights for manufacturing companies seeking to improve environmental performance, strengthen ESG implementation, and build sustainable competitive advantage through leadership-driven innovation and organisational transformation.
team_leader: Greeni Maheshwari, Richard Ramsawak

title: Perceptions, Information, and Decisions in Health Tourism
description:
Health tourism has emerged as a key component of global health and wellness systems, encompassing preventive, therapeutic, and rehabilitative services that motivate individuals to travel across borders. While health tourism offers benefits such as improved well-being, cost efficiency, and access to specialised services, decision-making in this context remains complex. Individuals must balance perceived risks, anticipated benefits, and sustainability considerations related to patient safety, ethical practices, information transparency, and long-term health outcomes. Existing health tourism research often focuses on economic impacts or experiential outcomes, with limited integration of digital behavioural evidence and sustainability perspectives. 

This research examines how health tourists evaluate risks and benefits in their decision-making processes and how sustainability-related considerations shape trust, information-seeking behaviour, provider choice, and intention to engage in health tourism. Drawing on literature from health tourism, healthcare services, consumer decision-making, and information systems, the study aims to develop a comprehensive framework capturing both perceptual and behavioural dimensions of health tourism decision-making. 

A mixed-methods research design will be employed. The first phase involves a structured, theory-driven review of health tourism and related literature to identify key constructs and research gaps. The second phase applies machine learning techniques to large-scale digital trace data, including online reviews, social media discussions, and health tourism platforms, to uncover latent themes, sentiment patterns, and behavioural signals. Methods such as topic modelling, text classification, and sentiment analysis will be used. The final phase integrates qualitative interviews and a quantitative survey to validate and refine the proposed framework across different health tourism contexts.
team_leader: Phuong Hoang, Huy Truong

title: RURAL LABOR TRANSFORMATION IN VIETNAM: THE ROLES OF RESOURCES, CLIMATE SHOCKS, AND DIGITALISATION
description:
This project investigates how rural households in Vietnam reallocate labor between farm and off-farm activities in response to three transformative forces: (i) access to resources, (ii) climate shocks, and (iii) digitalisation. Rural labor shifts are well documented descriptively, but little causal evidence exists on how these drivers shape household strategies and resilience.

The research will provide new insights into:
1.	How land, credit, education, and networks enable households to diversify into non-farm employment.
2.	How climatic shocks, such as floods, rainfall anomalies, and temperature extremes, affect labor allocation and adaptation.
3.	How digital technologies influence labor transitions, income diversification, and resilience.
By combining existinghousehold surveys with climate and ICT data, the study will generate causal evidence on the drivers of rural transformation in an emerging economy context.

The project will produce a PhD thesis comprising three empirical papers, with findings submitted to leading journals in development, labor, agricultural, or digital economics. Results will inform digital inclusion strategies, climate adaptation programs, and resource policies to enhance rural livelihoods.

Applicants should have a master’s degree in economics, development economics, or related fields, strong econometric skills, and research interests in labour markets, climate or digitalisation.

The successful candidate, who must be based at one of RMIT’s two Vietnam campuses, will be awarded a four-year tuition waiver and a stipend of approximately VND 170 million per year. Receipt of this scholarship is conditional upon the candidate committing to working full-time on her/his thesis and undertaking the RMIT College of Business and Law’s PhD course sequence in their first year of study.
team_leader: Trinh Nguyen,  Bob Baulch

title: The Role of AI-Integrated Content in Shaping Brand Image through Multimodal Communication
description:
Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming the digital communication landscape, with generative AI becoming a central force in how content is created and consumed. According to Gartner (2023), nearly one-third of marketing communications from major brands is now AI-generated, and by 2030, AI is expected to power 90% of all online content, fundamentally reshaping consumer interactions with media. 
As brands increasingly automate communication using AI, the need for authentic, engaging, and trustworthy content through various multimodality and channels has become prevalent as it might influence customers’ brand attitude and perception of brand authenticity, brand image, and brand personality (Kirkby et al., 2023; Yuan et al., 2023; Park and Ahn, 2024). Brand authenticity, personality, and image have been widely studied in both traditional marketing and digital settings. However, the rise of AI-generated content across various modalities create new dynamics of perception, trust, and engagement with brand image/personality which remains under-researched (Ali et al., 2025). 
This research project aims to investigate how AI-generated content across different modalities—linguistic, visual, gestural, spatial, and audio—can influence consumer perception of brand authenticity, brand personality, and brand image in the digital era.
team_leader:
Phuong Hoang, Anh Nguyen
Jasper Teow

title: Human Artificial Intelligence in Influencer Marketing to Predict Consumer Journey
description: This study investigates the combined impact of artificial intelligence (AI)-enabled marketing technologies (MarTech) and influencer marketing campaigns on consumer attitudes, trust, and purchase intentions across the whole consumer decision-making process. It focuses on human-AI collaboration rather than full automation, and looks into how data-driven targeting, personalised content, and influencer credibility interact to impact awareness, evaluation, and conversion phases in both online and offline marketplaces. Recognising that consumers respond differently to AI-personalised material, this study investigates the drivers of acceptance and the causes of uncertainty. It also investigates how demographic factors such as age, cultural background, and digital literacy influence these outcomes, as understanding these interactions is critical for brands seeking to maximise client involvement while maintaining authenticity and trust. The study design aims to rely on a mixed-methods approach. In the qualitative phase, in-depth interviews with industry practitioners and customers are to be conducted to uncover decision triggers, perceived risks, and strategies for developing trust. The gathered insights shall inspire the creation of a large-scale quantitative survey, including AI-driven sentiment analysis and predictive modeling. That would enable more accurate detection of behavioural patterns and forecasting of decision consequences. The study findings are expected to provide practical strategies for brands, MarTech developers, and policymakers to design ethical, adaptive, and hyper-performance marketing ecosystems. By linking theoretical knowledge with practical application, the study aims to develop actionable frameworks for providing personalised, trustworthy, and impactful marketing experiences that resonate with customers and enhance brand loyalty across industries by combining human judgment with AI-driven efficiency.
team_leader: Agnis Stibe,  Anushka Siriwardana

title: FinTech and Sustainable Finance
description: The financial system is undergoing rapid transformation driven by advances in Financial Technology (FinTech). At the same time, the urgent need to address global sustainability challenges—climate change, biodiversity loss, social inequality—requires a fundamental rethinking of how capital is mobilised, allocated, and monitored. This project explores the intersection between FinTech and Sustainable Finance, investigating how emerging technologies such as blockchain, artificial intelligence, decentralised finance (DeFi), green digital assets, and impact-driven digital platforms can accelerate the transition towards a low-carbon, inclusive economy. The project will examine how FinTech innovations and adoption can (1) improve transparency, traceability, and accountability in sustainable finance (e.g., blockchain-enabled ESG reporting, green bonds tracking), (2) enhance capital mobilisation for sustainability goals through novel financing mechanisms (e.g., peer-to-peer lending for renewable projects, tokenised carbon credits), (3) support inclusive and equitable financial access, particularly for underserved communities and regions vulnerable to climate risks, (4) integrate data analytics and AI to assess environmental, social, and governance (ESG) impacts in real-time, enabling more efficient and responsible investment decisions and (5) assess how FinTech and ESG adoption influences firms' behaviour and capital allocation decisions, including risk–return trade-offs, portfolio diversification, and willingness to pay for environmental and social impact. The research may adopt a combination of empirical quantitative analysis of financial and ESG datasets, case studies of FinTech applications in sustainability-focused markets, and policy and regulatory analysis in different jurisdictions. This project will contribute to academic literature at the frontier of FinTech and Sustainable Finance while delivering actionable insights for industry, policymakers, and technology developers. The findings aim to inform the design of financial systems that are both technologically advanced and environmentally/socially responsible—helping to meet the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

title: Smart and Sustainable Cities in ASEAN: Leadership and Collaboration through the Triple Helix Framework
description:
By 2050, more than two-thirds of the world's population will live in cities, making urban development a global priority. The combination of rapid urbanization and advance technologies of the Fourth Industrial Revolution has driven the rise of smart sustainable cities trend worldwide.

For ASEAN, developing smart sustainable cities is an urgent need and a major challenge, as the region must balance economic growth with protecting its low-lying coastal cities from climate change. Although the ASEAN Smart Cities Network was launched in 2018, its progress has been slow. While the Triple Helix model—which promotes collaboration between universities, industry, and government—has been explored for smart city development, its application in ASEAN remains underexplored. Furthermore, Existing studies often use this model to assess performance but fail to analyze how leadership and collaboration truly influence outcomes.

This research will address this gap by examining the Triple Helix model in selected ASEAN cities. Through a comparative case study approach, it will investigate the model's current status, including type of modifications, compare it to Western cases, and analyze how local factors either help or hinder its development. The study will use document analysis, interviews, and cross-case analysis. Ultimately, this research will both expand the Triple Helix framework to the ASEAN context and offer practical policy insights to drive inclusive, sustainable urban development and regional innovation.
team_leader: Greeni Maheshwari, Trung Nguyen, Burkhard Schrage, Greeni Maheshwari

title: Leveraging Digital Technologies to Influence Consumer Behavior in Sustainable Fashion
description:
Project Aims
The aim of the research project is to investigate how digital technologies such as Internet of Things (IoT), artificial intelligence (AI), blockchain, augmented reality (AR), and virtual reality (VR), can be leveraged to shape, reinforce, and measure sustainable consumer behavior in the fashion industry. The study will focus on identifying psychological, behavioral, and technological factors that encourage the adoption of sustainable fashion practices and support the transition toward circular business models.

Project Gap
Consumer behaviour in sustainable fashion context has received much attention from past academics and researchers. However, with the new generation of consumers placing great importance on sustainable consumption, new knowledge to the customer attitude-behaviour gap is still in need (Busalim, Ghabban, and Hussin, 2022).  Ko and Murgia (2024) highlighted the rise of research on AI, VR, AR, virtual try-on, metaverse, and their applications in the fashion design and fashion marketing industry, and suggested future research to continue exploring the impact of these technologies on customer-brand relationship using innovative theories. Sinha, Sharma, and Agrawal (2023) emphasized a need to integrate micro-level consumer sensitivity factors and personalized engagement methods with macro-level circular economy strategies. According to the authors, existing studies often isolate consumer behavior from digital ecosystems, neglecting how digital technologies and digital platforms affect trust, transparency, and identity alignment in sustainable purchasing behavior. Future project proposals can seek to address these gaps by exploring how digital technologies can activate, personalize, and scale sustainable consumer engagement within a circular fashion model.
Some research questions to be considered:
•	How do digital technologies such as Internet of Things (IoT), artificial intelligence (AI), blockchain, augmented reality (AR), and virtual reality (VR), influence consumer attitudes, identity alignment, and purchase intentions toward sustainable fashion?
•	What roles do social factors—such as social influence, self-concept, and perceived warmth—play in shaping consumer behavior in digitally mediated environments such as social media, e-commerce, or mobile app?
•	How can digital interventions (e.g., transparency tools, gamification, personalized communication) be designed to promote circular fashion practices and reduce the attitude-behavior gap in sustainable fashion consumption?
Methodology
This project should employ a mixed-method approach to explore the various facets of the research questions.
team_leader: Umair Akram; Nguyen Thi Van Anh

title: Harmonizing Trade Standards in Asia: Measuring Economic Impact of Non-Tariff Measures
description:
Asia, known for its dynamic economies, has rapidly deepened its integration into the global markets. Regions such as South Asia and ASEAN have played pivotal roles in this process. Through trade agreements, such as the Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership (CPTPP), tariff barriers have been significantly reduced. However, non-tariff measures (NTMs) have emerged as more formidable trade barriers than tariff measures. These include diverse product standards; testing and certification regimes; and customs procedures aimed at ensuring health, safety, and environmental protection. However, their lack of harmonization across countries leads to duplication, higher costs, and shipment delays, undermining exporter competitiveness and broader welfare gains from integration.
This PhD research project aimed to quantify the economic impact of NTMs on trade in Asia using a computable general equilibrium (CGE) model calibrated to the Global Trade Analysis Project (GTAP) database. This study identifies the most trade-restrictive NTMs affecting key sectors such as electronics, textiles and garments, agri-food, and processed foods. It also simulates scenarios involving mutual recognition agreements, adoption of international standards, and streamlined border procedures.
This research seeks to facilitate deeper and nuanced policy dialogue on trade integration and sustainable development goals (SDG) 17 (Revitalize the global partnership for sustainable development), specifically targeting goals 17.10, 17.11 and 17.12 (focused on trade). By analyzing the impact of NTM reforms on trade flows, sectoral output, and economic welfare, this study provides new evidence to guide regulatory convergence, reform priorities, and Asia’s evolving role in value chains.
Potential candidates interested in embarking on this project can contact the principal supervisor, Dr. Adeel Ahmed via email: adeel.ahmed@rmit.edu.vn for further information.
team_leader: Adeel Ahmed,  Ronald Kumar, Tung Bui

title: Artificial Intelligence in MarTech, Influencer Marketing, Consumer Behavior, and Decision Journey
description: This research examines the interplay between marketing technology (MarTech), influencer marketing, consumer behavior, and the customer decision journey, with a forward-looking integration of human-artificial intelligence principles. It bridges the gap between human and machine capabilities, emphasizing collaboration and augmentation rather than replacement. It offers groundbreaking potential to transform consumer interactions and decision-making in digital marketing environments. As digital platforms evolve, understanding these dimensions is essential for refining strategies, improving engagement, and fostering AI-driven hyper-performance in marketing. MarTech and influencer marketing are revolutionizing brand-consumer interactions. However, when augmented by human-AI systems, more research is needed to evaluate their combined effectiveness across the customer decision process. This research investigates how MarTech tools and influencer strategies shape consumer behavior and decision-making at each journey stage. Key questions include: How can MarTech and influencer marketing transform decision-making? What factors influence consumer acceptance and resistance, especially with AI-augmented insights? How do these integrated approaches impact purchase decisions across demographic groups? How can we foster hyper-performance in the contemporary marketing environment? This study analyzes consumer interactions with MarTech and influencer materials to build predictive consumer behavior models. The mixed-methods methodology combines qualitative interviews with experts and quantitative surveys enriched by AI-driven sentiment analysis and predictive algorithms to identify patterns and trends. This research provides critical insights for marketers, developers, and policymakers, enabling more precise, adaptive marketing strategies. By linking theoretical knowledge with practical application, the study aims to enhance understanding of how digital tools redefine consumer behavior and decision-making in transformative ways.
team_leader: Agnis Stibe, Anushka Siriwardana

title: Designing Sustainable Digital Transformation Pathways for SMEs
description:
We invite applications for a PhD research project focused on how small and medium enterprises (SMEs) in Vietnam can develop the capability required for sustainable digital transformation through the adoption of artificial intelligence (AI), supported by greater awareness and access to public policy initiatives. As core drivers of emerging economies such as Vietnam, SMEs need to strengthen their readiness to respond to the challenges and opportunities of a rapidly evolving digital landscape. 
Despite the growing emphasis on digital transformation, there remains a limited amount of empirical research on how Vietnamese SMEs can develop the specific capabilities required for AI-driven change. In particular, the intersection of internal capabilities and public policy engagement is underexplored. The project will investigate key management capabilities that enable effective AI integration, including innovation readiness, digital adaptability, strategic leadership, and data-informed decision-making. Organizational enablers such as workforce development and learning systems will also be considered as part of the broader capability landscape required for transformation. 
In addition, the research will examine how SMEs engage with existing government programs and incentives designed to support digital innovation. It will assess the role of awareness, accessibility, and institutional alignment in enabling SMEs to fully leverage these initiatives. 
This study contributes to the national agenda for digital advancement and inclusive economic growth of an emerging nation like Vietnam. It offers a timely opportunity for interdisciplinary research at the intersection of management, technology adoption, and public policy, generating actionable insights for SME leaders, policymakers, and the broader innovation ecosystem.
team_leader: Divya Juneja, Santiago Velasquez

title: From Chalkboards to Innovation: A Study of Vietnamese Academic Entrepreneurship in the Digital Era
description:
As Vietnam undergoes rapid digital transformation, the higher education sector is also evolving. While digital entrepreneurship has attracted global attention, limited research has examined how these shifts influence academic behaviour in emerging economies. Vietnam’s dynamic digital reform agenda and evolving policy environment provide a valuable context for exploring these developments.

This research aims to investigate how university academics in Vietnam engage in entrepreneurial activities in response to emerging digital technologies such as artificial intelligence, big data, and digital platforms. It seeks to understand the factors that shape these behaviours, including individual motivations, institutional environments, and national-level reforms. The study will adopt a mixed-methods approach, beginning with a quantitative phase involving a survey of approximately 400 academics. This will be followed by a qualitative phase, consisting of in-depth interviews with around 20 purposively selected participants, continuing until thematic saturation is reached.

Theoretically, this study contributes to an integrated, multilevel understanding of academic entrepreneurship in a digitally transforming, non-Western context, highlighting the interplay of individual, institutional, and national forces. It offers a contextualised framework that views academic entrepreneurship as a socially embedded process. Practically, the study will provide evidence-based recommendations to policymakers and university leaders in Vietnam to foster inclusive, innovation-oriented academic environments aligned with the country’s broader digital and developmental goals.
team_leader: Greeni Maheshwari, Yin Teng Chew,  Trang LE

title: Digital transformation and Vietnam’s niche and emerging industries
description:
This project focuses on digital transformation’s impact on Vietnam’s niche and emerging industries. Vietnam is both a leader and as the potential to excel in international markets. First, Vietnam ranks second in the world in coffee exports by volume (Statista, 2024a, Statista, 2024b). As documented in some cases, Vietnam is also increasingly the home of entrepreneurial firms that are adding value to coffee production, including by using technologies (Viet Nam News, 2023). Second, Vietnam is also one of the world’s leaders in cashew nut production and exports (Statista, 2024c). In this industry, Vietnam is also a major cashew nut importer that, subsequently exports these products to other markets (Chi et al., 2018). Third, the potential for Vietnam’s organic food production to enter lucrative markets, particularly the European Union (EU) market, is considerable (VCCI, 2023). Recent new legislation (VCCI, 2024) concerning the issuance of organic certificates to be able to export into the EU presents both challenges and opportunities for Vietnam. Moreover, with the EU seeking to promote technology transfer, digitalisation and high-tech transformation of production processes (VCCI, 2024) there are important implications for organic producers. 

This project proposes various research questions (RQs), including but not limited to the following:
RQ1: How are firms benefitting from digital transformation, including with regard to their supply chain (e.g., production, logistics, adding value, packaging, selling/exporting).
RQ2: What opportunities/challenges do they perceive from a deeper involvement in digitalisation/digital transformation practices?
RQ3: How do they perceive their industry’s future regarding the adoption of digitalisation/digital transformation?
team_leader: Oanh Thi Kim Vu, Abel Duarte Alonso,Seng Kok

title: An examination of businesses operating in smart city environments
description:
Smart cities can provide advantages to government and business entities in the form of efficiency, connectivity, smart transportation, security, cost savings or smart buildings (Wirsbinna & Grega, 2021). The smart city phenomenon is changing from a predominant focus on urban infrastructure development (2000-2015) to a stronger focus on services (Kim, 2022). This shift suggests an increasing importance of smart city maintenance and operation rather than building, where smart technologies, big data, artificial intelligence, and urban data are leveraged to offer uninterrupted and valuable services to smart city residents (Kim, 2022).

However, an analysis or overview of the effects of smart cities, both positive and negative, is lacking (Lim et al., 2019). In addition, research focusing on smart cities’ unbalanced effects of technology diffusion on various groups (e.g., residents and disadvantaged groups) and institutions, including small businesses) is limited (Zhao et al., 2021). Similarly, there is a dearth of smart city investigations focusing on the business perspective (Pašalić et al., 2021). This gap is also obvious in the case of Vietnam and other economies, both established and emerging. The project embraces a number of foci and directions to examine research questions  that illuminate the understanding of businesses operating in smart city ecosystems, including but not limited to the following:

How has the business experienced the different phases of smart city development?
How has the smart city regime affected the local businesses operating in this ecosystem?
How do business owners/managers perceive the future coexistence with the smart city regime?

title: Customer Engagement with Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Marketing Strategies
description:
Project Aims
This project aims to understand factors influencing customer perceptions and engagement with AI-powered tools and content currently being used by brands and marketers. The project addresses the following questions:
• How customers perceive the information quality performance of AI-generated content and AI-powered tools?
• Which factors contribute to customers’ trust and satisfaction with information quality from AI-powered content and tools, therefore determining their engagement with the content and the tools?
• What strategies are required to enhance customer engagement with AI-content and tools?
Project Gap
The application of Artificial Intelligence (AI) has become more popular in marketing recently as it enables hyper-personalized experiences, predictive analytics, and real-time customer engagement. AI-driven innovations allow automated and personalised content creation with information provided to customers in real-time, prompting them to interact and engage with AI-powered tools for better service quality. However, the effectiveness of AI in engaging with customers depends on customer perception of information quality, perceived interactivity, and perceived personalization from AI-generated content and AI-powered tools. Other factors might include privacy concerns, trust, behavioural factors such as customer learning experience, or generational, cultural, or socioeconomic factors (Suraña‐Sánchez and Aramendia‐Muneta, 2024). Understanding how these factors influence the trust and satisfaction of AI-generated content and tools can suggest better marketing strategies to enhance customer engagement and business marketing performance. 
Methodology
This project should employ a mixed-method approach to explore the various facets of the research questions.
Suraña‐Sánchez, C. and Aramendia‐Muneta, M.E., 2024. Impact of artificial intelligence on customer engagement and advertising engagement: A review and future research agenda. International Journal of Consumer Studies, 48(2), p.e13027.
team_leader: Agnis Stibe

title: Sustainable Finance and Market Stability: Unveiling the Role of Green Investments
description:
The proposed project aims to explore the relationship dynamics between sustainable investments and financial market trends. The study will focus on various sustainable financial investments such as ESG securities, green bonds, social bonds, Green Loans, ESG Funds, Green Exchange-Traded Funds, Carbon Credits, Sustainable Real Estate Investment Trusts, and Green Insurance Products with regard to their impact on financial markets and economies. Given that existing literature in this domain is still developing, there is a considerable research gap regarding the comprehensive influence of sustainable investments on financial market performance. This project seeks to fill that gap by thoroughly examining the role of sustainable financial products in promoting financial market stability and understanding their impact on market dynamics.
Utilizing a quantitative research methodology, this study will analyze data from companies and economies that issue sustainable securities, encompassing both developed and emerging markets. The project’s goal is to provide valuable insights into how sustainable investments align with financial market advancements, contributing to broader sustainable development and climate action objectives. These findings will offer critical opportunities for investors and policymakers to support and capitalize on sustainable practices.
team_leader: Fiza Qureshi

title: Permeation of robotic technology in the Vietnamese hospitality cultural norms
description:
The tourism and hospitality industry (T&H) is on the cusp of embracing robotic (AI). There are many opportunities with robotics AI which can lead to a possible change of attitudes in guest experience and service operations.  In Vietnam there is a foreseeable integration of these technologies with the current cultural norms (Chung et al., 2019).

While these technologies are seen to be effective from an operational perspective, its proliferation can have a profound effect on a culturally rich country i.e. Vietnam (research gap).  As such it would be great interest to undertake study on the integration of such robotics technologies vis-à-vis on culturally norm in a hospitality operational trade in a defined Vietnamese city, in terms of local service standards, kinematics, time-motion functions, service design standards from a local service provider standpoint to a user perspective. No such research or study has been conducted within Vietnam yet.

The overarching research question is to determine the acceptance of robotic within the framework of Belance, Carlo and Scheper (2019)’s robotic integration for Vietnamese T&H operators and its impacts on the above 4 factors vis-à-vis its cultural norms from different stakeholders.  This is in-line with RMIT’s Vietnam research interest in Smart Cities and Innovative Industries.

The impacts of a research will grant T&H companies to pivot their operational functions towards a more robotic inclined industry accordingly to the users in line with industry and the nation’s desire.  It will also create springboards for ancillary hospitality to navigate the use of this new technologies.
team_leader: Justin Matthew Pang, Daisy Kanagasapapathy, Ushik Shrestha Khwakhali

title: AI Mentors in Education: Impact on Staff Behaviour, Agency, Wellbeing
description:
1. Project Aim
The purpose of this project is to investigate the impact of AI chatbots functioning as mentors on educational staff's behaviour, agency, and overall wellbeing. Ultimately, the study seeks to assess whether AI chatbots can serve as effective mentors and support systems for staff, thereby improving working conditions in the educational environment, where time constraints, a focus on students’ results, and daily interactions with students and parents often limit teachers’ agency and exhaust them emotionally.

2. Project Gap
While the use of AI chatbots in education has predominantly focused on student learning and support, there is a notable lack of management research examining their role in mentoring educational staff. Existing studies often overlook the potential benefits and challenges that AI chatbot mentors may present to staff members' professional development and wellbeing within organisational settings. This project addresses this gap by exploring (i) how AI chatbot mentorship affects staff behaviours and professional practices; (ii) the influence of AI chatbots on staff agency and wellbeing.

3. Methodology
A mixed-methods approach will be used. Structured questionnaires will collect quantitative data on changes in staff behaviour, perceived agency, and wellbeing as a result of interactions with AI chatbot mentors. Semi-structured interviews will provide qualitative insights into personal experiences and attitudes towards AI mentorship. Chat logs will be analysed to identify themes and patterns correlating with changes in professional practices and decision-making processes. Triangulating these data enables holistic answers to the research questions.
team_leader: Adriel Sim, Nhu Do
for_codes: 350507 Workplace wellbeing and quality of working life (45%)

title: Employee wellbeing and engagement in human resource management
description: Technology advancements and the rapid changes of socio-economic factors have implications on human resource management, particularly employee wellbeing and employee engagement. Employee well-being is strongly associated with business-unit level profitability and negatively correlated with turnover (Krekel et al., 2019). It can be noted that employees’ well-being, which can be measured by job satisfaction, has been claimed to result in high productivity and low turnover. However, Krekel et al. (2019) show that emotional and cognitive elements such as happiness-enhancing treatment and employee engagement also affect employee productivity.  Extensive training, participation and communication, sensitive selection, and incentive compensation are found positively related to employee engagement in service industry in India through employees’ vigour (i.e high energy and mental resilience at work), dedication, and absorption (i.e. high concentration at work) (Goyal and Patwardhan, 2020). Work satisfaction and rewards are mainly antecedents of employee engagement at small and medium enterprises in Indonesia, while remuneration satisfaction, self-leadership, and interpersonal leadership positively influenced employee engagement in Thailand (Rakthin et al., 2021; Dyah Sugandini et al, 2018). Boccoli and colleagues (2023) reported organizations play an important role in determining the level of employee engagement when implementing policies and activities within the organizational scope. This proposal calls for PhD students who are interested in investigating employee wellbeing and employee engagement, using either quantitative or qualitative or mixed methods to address the unanswered questions of human resource management in the contemporary workplace.

title: The AI Awakening: Transforming Vietnam's Psychological Landscape
description:
Artificial intelligence (AI) integration into workplaces presents a transformative shift in work, impacting employees' psychological well-being and organizational dynamics. In Vietnam, where rapid technological advancements coincide with cultural nuances, understanding the relationship between AI and psychological capital is vital. Psychological capital encompasses positive psychological resources such as resilience, optimism, self-efficacy, and hope, which play a crucial role in individuals' ability to navigate challenges and thrive in the workplace. This research proposal aims to investigate how AI integration influences psychological capital among employees in Vietnam, identifying mechanisms and interventions to support their well-being and productivity.

The proposed research will employ a mixed-methods approach, utilizing surveys and semi-structured interviews to collect data from employees across various industries in Vietnam. Quantitative surveys will assess the impact of AI integration on psychological capital, measuring factors such as resilience, optimism, self-efficacy, and hope. Qualitative interviews will provide deeper insights into the mechanisms through which AI affects psychological capital, considering cultural, organizational, and individual perspectives.

By examining the relationship between AI integration and psychological capital in Vietnam, this research seeks to contribute both academically and practically. The findings will inform organizations and policymakers about the potential psychological effects of AI integration and identify strategies to cultivate a positive work environment conducive to employees' well-being and performance. Additionally, the research aims to highlight the importance of fostering psychological capital in the face of technological advancements, promoting resilience and adaptability among the Vietnamese workforces.
team_leader: Adriel Sim. Aemin Nasir, Nhu Do
for_codes: 350507        Workplace wellbeing and quality of working life 50%

title: Sustainability, not Achilles’ heel of digital transformation
description: This research project aims to reconcile the two seemingly contracting trends, “digital transformation and sustainability”, exploring how digital transformation and sustainability can be synergized within business ecosystems. It will involve an extensive review and analysis of current digital transformation strategies and sustainability practices across various industries, e.g., healthcare, logistics, and marketing. The primary objective is identifying and developing integrated approaches to drive digital innovation and sustainable development. The project will propose models and frameworks encapsulating best practices, guiding businesses to leverage digital technologies to enhance their sustainability goals. Potential topic(s) can explore the synergy between digitization and sustainability, the transformative potential of digital technologies in achieving sustainability in supply chain operations, or how technologies can be integrated with sustainable practices to create resilient and eco-friendly supply chains. Applicants for this project will be based on the RMIT Vietnam campus but will be enrolled through RMIT Australia as offshore candidates.
team_leader: Phuong Hoang, Huy Truong , Van-Anh Nguyen
for_codes: 350303 Business information systems (25%) , 350304 Business systems in context (25%), 350705 Innovation management (25%), 350307 Technology management (25%)

title: Gender Equality, Disability, Social Inclusion (GEDSI) and Corporate Performance of SMEs in Vietnam
description:
This research investigates the impact of Gender Equality, Disability, and Social Inclusion (GEDSI) on firm performance in small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in Vietnam. SMEs play a critical role in Vietnam’s economic development, yet there is limited research on how implementing inclusive practices influences business outcomes. This study aims to fill that gap by examining how GEDSI initiatives (i.e. promoting gender equality, accommodating disabilities, and fostering social inclusion) impact firm performance measures such as productivity and profitability. 

By conducting surveys and interviews with SME owners, managers, and employees across various industries, this research will use primary data and a qualitative/ quantitative analysis approach to identify the key factors that support or hinder the adoption of GEDSI practices. If possible, secondary data will also be used to identify the GEDSI impacts on firm financial performance and productivity. The findings will provide actionable insights for SMEs on how inclusivity can enhance their competitiveness, while also contributing to broader social goals. 

The research aligns with several Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) including SDG 5 - Gender Equality, SDG 8 - Decent Work and Economic Growth, and SDG 10 - Reduced Inequality. This research aims to provide evidence-based recommendations to promote inclusivity and sustainable growth in Vietnam’s SME sector.
team_leader: Dao Le Trang Anh, Dang Thao Quyen

title: Development Finance and Demographic Dividends in an Interconnected World
description:
Countries worldwide are undergoing demographic transitions marked by declining fertility and mortality rates. These transitions generate first and second demographic dividends (DDs) with implications for growth and development. The first dividend emerges from lower dependency ratios, freeing resources for consumption and investment, while the second arises from increased savings and human capital accumulation as workforces age.

DDs are not automatic but represent "windows of opportunity" requiring deliberate policy interventions. In our interconnected world, international processes complement domestic efforts in DD realization. While trade and labour mobility have been explored, evidence on development finance flows, particularly FDI and aid, remains limited.

This project will use micro- and macro-level data from international sources to examine how bilateral FDI flows contribute to realization of the DD, plus the mediating role of foreign aid. A three to four-paper quantitative thesis is envisaged.
The project contributes to the debate on middle-income ‘traps’ and ‘growing old before getting rich’, while informing policies for aging populations, inclusive growth, and sustainable development.

Applicants should have a strong background in development economics and applied statistics/econometrics. Knowledge of demography, international and labour economics is an advantage. 

The student appointed will join the Development Economics research cluster in RMIT Vietnam and the cross-campus RMIT Centre for International Development. S/he will be encouraged to present papers based on her/his research at national and international conferences and submit. manuscripts based his/her doctoral research to A/Q1 ranked journals
team_leader: Bob Baulch, Longfeng Ye

title: AI-Adoption and Digital Industrialisation in Southeast Asia Economies
description:
Southeast Asian economies—such as Vietnam, Indonesia, and Malaysia—are undergoing rapid structural transformation as they deepen their participation in global value chains and shift toward higher-value-added activities. Despite this, many Asian economies remain vulnerable to the middle-income trap, where rising labour costs erode competitiveness without corresponding gains in productivity and innovation. Artificial Intelligence (AI) presents a critical opportunity to overcome these constraints by enhancing efficiency, enabling digital industrialization, and supporting innovation-led growth. Applications in advanced analytics, agritech, and smart logistics are already reshaping key sectors. However, AI adoption across ASEAN remains uneven, constrained by gaps in digital infrastructure, limited cloud readiness, weak data governance, shortages of skilled labour, and fragmented institutional support. While governments have introduced national AI strategies, firms’ ability to effectively adopt and operationalize these technologies remains uncertain.
This study examines the determinants and impacts of AI adoption at both firm and country levels. It investigates key technological, institutional, human capital, and socio-cultural factors that influence adoption. Additionally, how patterns differ across countries and sectors, and how AI affects productivity, innovation, and digital industrialization. It also explores policy interventions that can support AI-driven leapfrogging and facilitate the transition to innovation-led growth.
Although existing frameworks such as Diffusion of Innovations, TAM, and UTAUT provide useful insights, they are rarely applied to AI in emerging Asian contexts. This study addresses this gap by providing new empirical evidence using advanced analytical methods, offering actionable insights for policymakers and firms while contributing to broader development goals, including SDGs 8 and 9.
team_leader: Richard Ramsawak, Duong Vu, Adeel Ahmed

title: Digitalization, Demographic Change, And Health: Understanding Technology’s Role in Shaping Population Well-Being
description:
Digital transformation is revolutionizing healthcare systems across the world. Many countries are experiencing profound demographic transitions, due to population ageing, declining fertility, changing migration patterns, and widening inequalities. These forces will have implications on individuals' ability to access health services and respond to new challenges.
The research project aims to explore how digitalization (including access to technology) interacts with demographic dynamics to influence health outcomes and health equity, particularly in emerging economies. Part of the research agenda will be to analyze multidimensional linkages between digitalization, demography, and health outcomes, with the view to proposing robust policies in the health sector. The study will draw on micro-level survey data related to health alongside macro-level time series data from national and international sources.
Research questions central to the project includes: (a) How digital readiness and ICT infrastructure affect population health and health system performance; (b) How demographic ageing, fertility, and migration moderate the impacts of digital transformation on health outcomes; and (c) how digital inclusion, literacy, and access shape inequalities in health and well-being. Researchers working on this project will be expected to contribute to broader objectives of achieving health resilience and social sustainability in a well-defined development context.
Applicants with a master's degree in applied economics, health economics, econometrics/data science, demographics/population studies, public health or development studies are encouraged to apply. Candidates should have proficiency in handling micro-level surveys and using econometric and statistical tools like STATA, R, or Python.
team_leader: Deepak Kumar Behera, Ronald Ravinesh Kumar
for_codes: 380108 Health Economics (40) 420302 Digital Transformation and Technology Economics (30) 440407 Socio-Economic Development (30)

title: Leveraging Big Data, and Machine Learning for Urban Development
description:
Urban growth among Southeast Asian countries is continuing to advance rapidly. Latest projections point to an increase in the percentage of urban population from 44% in 2010 to almost 66% by 2050 (Nations 2018).  Much of this growth has stemmed from the rapid industrialization, and economic advancement among key Asian countries such as Vietnam, Malaysia, and Thailand (Huff 2012, Fan, Ouyang et al. 2019). While this has brought benefits of increasing income, employment, and innovation, it has also created new challenges in urban planning and greater environmental risk to these locations.
This project aims to harness novel big data sources combined with advanced machine learning (ML) techniques to address urban development challenges in key Southeast Asian cities. For instance, data on real estate prices gathered from online sources can be combined with satellite imagery to develop elaborate random forest models, to analyze price trends, value new amenities such as green spaces, and assess the impact of policy changes. Near real-time mobility data can optimize transport networks and predict productivity and environmental benefits.  Satellite imagery combined with ecological and household data can be used to develop convolutional neural networks to evaluate the impacts of changes in built areas on the environment and to develop adaptive solutions. 
By focusing on cities like Ho Chi Minh City, Bangkok, and Manila, this research contributes to our understanding of the unique socio-economic characteristics of Southeast Asian cities and leverages new big data sources and techniques to develop innovative data-driven solutions to the challenges they face.
team_leader: Richard Ramsawak

title: Corporate governance and SDGs
description: The proposed project aims to empirically examine whether and how corporate governance mechanisms (e.g., board gender diversity, skills and education or background of the board members etc.) can be used to achieve one(s) of the sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). The literature in this strand is still growing to analyze the crucial role of corporate governance measures adopted by companies toward environmental sustainability. However, a comprehensive and compact research is needed to examine the role of corporate governance practices to achieve the desired outcomes. The proposed study will employ quantitative research approach using panel data of global firms (may include, developed and/or developing countries for comparative analysis). The proposed research will contribute to wider community i.e., researchers, policymakers, corporations, investors, and society at large by providing comprehensive understanding of the interplay of corporate governance practices and SDGs achievement, thereby contributing to the advancement of corporate sustainability globally.
team_leader: Irfan Haider

title: Local residents’ attitudes towards sustainable tourism development in post-Covid19 Vietnam: A case study from Ho Chi Minh City (project 1 of 2)
description: This project is part of a larger funded multidisciplinary project that addresses sustainable tourism development along the Kenh Te canal banks of Districts 4 and 7 in Saigon, Vietnam. This project seeks to address the impacts of (sustainable) tourism development in riverside areas of Saigon, concentrating on the impact on local residents’ lives and livelihoods. This study will address a gap in the sustainable tourism literature by investigating the impacts of tourism development in certain areas of Ho Chi Minh City that hitherto have had little to no contact with tourism. This study will also provide an additional testing ground for relationships between attitudes and intended behavior towards tourism development in Vietnam, which thus far has not been done. Findings will support researchers, policy makers, and tourism promotion campaigns. It will also enhance Vietnam’s tourism promotional efforts aimed at increasing revisits by international tourists. This study will use primarily a quantitative approach, seeking to establish the relationship between beliefs, attitudes, and intended behavior towards tourism development The study will use survey/questionnaire instruments of data collection and structural equation modelling (SEM) and multivariate analysis towards to investigate the nature and strength of relationships between tourism development, tourism developers, and the impact that tourism has/will have in local residents’ lives and livelihoods.
team_leader:
Nuno F. Ribeiro, Robert McClelland, Duc Trinh Tran, Catherine Earl
Marian Makkar, Andy Stiff

title: The impacts of Blockchain technology adoption on supply chain performance: an empirical study from Vietnam
description:
In recent years, blockchain technology has revolutionized supply chain management, enhancing efficiency and transparency with its decentralized, immutable ledger. Most existing studies on blockchain in supply chain management focus on theoretical benefits, with insufficient empirical data on its actual impact. Previous research primarily relies on literature reviews (Azzi et al., 2019; Longo et al., 2019; Nguyen et al., 2023; Han and Fang, 2024) and limited empirical analysis (Alazab, 2021; Karamchandani et al., 2021). Additionally, there is a lack of research on the unique challenges and opportunities of blockchain adoption in emerging markets like Vietnam. To the best of our knowledge, there are only few empirical studies that had focused on the specific context of emerging market like Vietnamese transport and logistics companies. Furthermore, existing studies either provide a composite view of supply chains or focus on individual stages, limiting detailed understanding. There is a need for stage-wise assessment of supply chain practices with blockchain integration to evaluate its direct impact on performance. Additionally, despite extensive literature on blockchain, few clear frameworks exist for deciding whether a supply chain should adopt blockchain. This study aims to develop such a framework, aiding decision-making before implementation. Given Vietnam's rapid economic growth and strategic importance in global supply chains, understanding how blockchain can enhance supply chain performance is crucial.
Research methodology:
•        Conduct a literature review on explore existing research on the impacts of blockchain technology adoption on supply chain performance, with a focus on the transport and logistics sectors. 
•        Develop a theoretical framework, regarding to the potential of blockchain to positively affect supply chain performance in terms of several indicators. 
•        Employ mixed-methods research, including qualitative interviews and thematic analysis, and quantitative surveys with Structural Equation Modelling, to test the proposed model 
•        Using primary data.
Expected outcomes:
We expect the study to uncover a comprehensive understanding of how blockchain technology impacts supply chain performance. It's providing valuable insights for businesses in blockchain technology adoption to enhance operational efficiency, shortens lead times, and optimizes inventory management, while also reducing transaction and operational costs. It improves supply chain visibility, ensuring product authenticity and stakeholder trust.

title: Understanding value creation from co-production between formal and informal waste collection within the circular economic framework
description:
The waste management sector in emergent countries represents a unique and complex environmental, social and economic challenge. Within the sector there has been a long-standing co-existence between the formal and informal waste collection systems. Much of the extant research has shown that informal waste collectors and recyclers can bring economic and social benefits, especially for vulnerable communities. This is premise upon its adapted coordination structure, minimal expenditures and the embrace for socio-economic opportunities (Wilson et al., 2006, Moreno-Sánchez et al., 2006). For example, plastics within the waste stream provide a distinct case where waste pickers or scavengers engage in selective waste collection services, potentially contributing to recycling efficiency, minimizing waste discharge into the wider environment. However, there are still substantial gaps within the extant academic literature exploring this socio-economic phenomenon, given the difficulties inherent with studies of informal waste-management, namely the diverse and unstructured contexts of waste-pickers. This has become an area of imperative interest especially considering the potential benefits and opportunities in furthering the circular economy discourse and impacts within Global South economies where much of these informal waste management systems exist ​(C. Joshi et al., 2019; Ono et al., 2023; Zisopoulos et al., 2023)​.  

Thus, the primary aim of this study is to better characterize and understand the plastic waste collection and recycling business model in a Global South economy, focusing on Vietnam. The study will disentangle the factors that contribute to value co-production between the informal and formal waste collection systems and its structure within a Vietnamese context. The research seeks to develop an analytical framework built on a system dynamics approach (Sterman et al., 2010; Estay-Ossandon et al., 2018) to explore (non-)market arrangements in the creation of informal and formal waste collection systems with the goal to improve the overall social, environmental and economic benefits furthering the circularity model.
team_leader: Kok Seng Kiong, Robert McClelland, Duc Trinh Tran

title: Large Language Models for Business Innovation and Decision-Making
description:
Financial markets aggregate vast quantities of information — corporate disclosures, macroeconomic data, news, and investor sentiment — into asset prices. Understanding how efficiently this process occurs, and how it can be supported or improved through AI, is one of the central questions of modern financial economics. This project investigates whether and how Large Language Models (LLMs) can advance our ability to predict asset prices, characterise investor behaviour, and assess the informational efficiency of financial markets.
Traditional asset price prediction models — from ARIMA and factor models to early deep learning approaches — have demonstrated predictive power in narrow conditions but struggle with the non-stationarity, regime changes, and behavioural dynamics that characterise real markets. LLMs offer a qualitatively different capability: the ability to process and interpret the unstructured textual information — analyst commentary, earnings call transcripts, news sentiment — that drives investor expectations and, ultimately, asset prices.
The efficient market hypothesis (EMH) holds that asset prices reflect all available information. Yet decades of empirical research in behavioural finance have documented systematic deviations from this ideal — momentum effects, overreaction and underreaction, herding, and sentiment-driven mispricing — that suggest markets are not fully efficient and that investor psychology plays a meaningful role in price formation.
This tension between market efficiency and behavioural anomalies is precisely where LLMs may offer the most valuable contributions. If asset prices are partly driven by how investors interpret and respond to information — rather than by the information itself — then AI systems that can model language, sentiment, and narrative at scale may capture predictive signals that traditional quantitative models miss. At the same time, the widespread adoption of LLM-based trading systems raises a deeper question: does AI adoption change the very market dynamics it seeks to predict? If LLMs become pervasive tools of investment analysis, their influence on investor behaviour and price formation could alter the efficiency properties of markets in ways that are not yet understood.
This project is motivated by the conviction that these are not merely technical questions but fundamental financial economics questions about the nature of information, the behaviour of investors, and the efficiency of markets in an AI-augmented era. The findings will be relevant to investors, regulators, and policymakers seeking to understand the implications of AI adoption for market structure and financial stability.
team_leader: Hà Xuân Sơn, AP. Binh Nguyen

title: Multi-AI Agents System for Adaptive Portfolio Management
description:
In the digital transformation era, artificial intelligence (AI) is reshaping global finance, notably through multi-agent systems (MAS) in portfolio management. MAS employ specialised AI agents—capable of technical analysis, sentiment detection, fundamental assessment, and reinforcement learning—to deliver adaptive investment solutions (Han et al., 2024; Espiga-Fernández et al., 2024; Huang & Tanaka, 2022). However, such AI adoption remains limited in emerging markets. Vietnam, a frontier market marked by low liquidity, retail dominance, and behavioural biases like herding and loss aversion (Cao et al., 2021; Nguyen, 2022), presents distinct challenges for MAS implementation. Standard AI models, often grounded in rational investor assumptions, may underperform in such contexts.

This research project aims to propose a behaviourally informed, context-aware MAS tailored to Vietnam’s investors. Incorporating behavioural finance, investorspecific constraints (e.g., risk tolerance, return expectations), and human-in-the-loop mechanisms for interpreting qualitative factors (e.g., policy changes), the solution aims to improve realism and responsiveness in volatile, sentiment-driven markets. Theoretically, the project refines market efficiency theory by quantifying behavioral impacts on AI-driven portfolio outcomes in frontier markets. Practically, it seeks to democratise access to sophisticated portfolio tools, enhance individual investor outcomes, and support scalable MAS deployment across emerging economies. Addressing a critical gap at the nexus of AI, finance, and behavioural science, this research holds relevance for scholars, fintech innovators, regulators, and investors pursuing inclusive and adaptive financial technologies.

HDR INDUSTRY PLACEMENT OPPORTUNITIES:
The project offers HDR industry placement opportunities across domains such as MAS development, policymaking on MAS use in finance, and designing incentive structures to improve investment behaviour, enhancing academic-industry collaboration and practical impact.

REFERENCES:
Han, X., Wang, N., Che, S., Yang, H., Zhang, K., & Xu, S. X. (2024, November). Enhancing investment analysis: Optimizing AI-Agent collaboration in financial research. In Proceedings of the 5th ACM International Conference on AI in Finance (pp. 538-546).
Espiga-Fernández, F., García-Sánchez, Á., & Ordieres-Meré, J. (2024). A Systematic Approach to Portfolio Optimization: A Comparative Study of Reinforcement Learning Agents, Market Signals, and Investment Horizons. Algorithms, 17(12), 570.
Huang, Z., & Tanaka, F. (2022). MSPM: A modularized and scalable multi-agent reinforcement learning-based system for financial portfolio management. Plos One, 17(2), e0263689.
Nguyen, T. (2022). A Study of Herding Behavior on Vietnam Stock Market. Journal of Economics, Finance and Accounting Studies, 4(4), 93-98.
Cao, M. M., Nguyen, N. T., & Tran, T. T. (2021). Behavioral factors on individual investors' decision making and investment performance: A survey from the Vietnam Stock Market. The Journal of Asian Finance, Economics and Business, 8(3), 845-853
team_leader: Duy Dang, Tuan Chu, Huy Doan

title: Digital Transformation in vibrant and emerging economies: Actions towards uncertainty
description:
This project aims to explore enablers and barriers towards digital transformation and adoption of emerging technologies (i.e. blockchain, artificial intelligence, Internet of Things (IoT)/sensors and cloud computing) in a dynamic Southeast-Asian market (i.e. Vietnam).

Amid the ongoing global trade tensions, operations of companies in Southeast Asia are impacted by changing relationships and dependencies on dominant markets such as China or the U.S. or seeking alternative partners in other countries. Simultaneously, lessons from COVID-19 pandemic exposing severe vulnerabilities in global supply chains accelerate the need for enhancing resilience, visibility, and operational agility to mitigate similar impacts in the future. As such, digital transformation plays a pivotal role in supporting these businesses to quickly capture and process information to respond to the above-mentioned uncertainty. In this scenario, Vietnam and its regional Southeast-Asian counterparts, as strategic nodes in a restructured global value chain, require significant preparation for digital transformation.

Potential research questions from this project include: what are the enablers and barriers for national strategies, infrastructure readiness, foreign direct investment, regulatory environments and organizational management to shape emerging technology adoption patterns in the current digital transformation landscape.

The research ideally adopts a mixed-methods approach, including surveys, ecosystem mapping, case studies, and policy analysis, to uncover both enablers and barriers to such digital transformation. By examining this evolving landscape, the project seeks to contribute actionable insights for policymakers and business leaders to navigate a future marked by trade disputes, health crises, and supply chain uncertainty.
team_leader: Thuan Nguyen Hoang, Chi Pham
for_codes: 350799 Strategy, management and organisational behaviour not elsewhere classified (40%)

title: Grasping artificial intelligence (AI) opportunities in higher education
description:
This project aims to explore the use of AI tools to support the learning of essential digital skills – i.e using ChatGPT to develop programming languages, and guide the use of data analytics platforms (PowerBI, SQL, Excel spreadsheets), and AI-based presentation slide generation platforms.

As AI and data-driven decision-making become critical in most fields, graduates and professionals across disciplines – particularly business and social sciences – can no longer rely solely on IT departments for ongoing support. There is a growing need for interdisciplinary education models that enable students to develop technical competencies independently. From a business school perspective, teaching AI and programming in an engaging, accessible manner still presents challenges, particularly for students without a technology background.

This project adopts a Design Science Research methodology and/or mixed-methods approach to design, implement, and evaluate intensive, hands-on courses tailored for non-technical students in public universities. We will design and conduct an intensive coursework teaching students to use AI-support tools to learn analytic software. We will observe and survey students’ performance, engagement, and perceived usefulness after completing the designed course. A key aspect of this project is understanding the learning characteristics and digital behavior of Generation Z, the dominant demographic in higher education. Known as digital natives, this generation adapts quickly to technology but requires interactive, personalized, and purpose-driven learning experiences. This study indicates how these interventions can inform broader curriculum strategies at public universities. Ultimately, the project aims to bridge the skill gap and prepare students for success in increasingly digital, interdisciplinary work environments.

Internship: The candidate can teach a short course in some public universities.
team_leader: Thuan Nguyen Hoang, Chi Pham
