title: Assessment of the Impact of Sand Mining on Sediment Transport in the Mekong River Basin
description:
The Mekong River basin represents one of Earth's most hydrologically complex and ecologically diverse systems. However, climate change, future dam construction, and sand mining threaten to negatively impact its sediment flux, crucial for the survival of its ecosystem. Up to 50% decline in suspended sediment flux has been predicted if all planned dams are built, potentially impacting local livelihoods and ecosystems (Bussi et al., 2020).
Sand mining operations exacerbate this impact and frequently create extraction pits 5-15m below the riverbed, significantly altering local hydraulic gradients and flow acceleration patterns. Consequently, channel incision has accelerated throughout the delta (Jordan et al. 2019).
This project employs Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) using Openfoam® to analyse the complex relationships between sediment transport, alterations in river morphology, and sand mining practices. Three-dimensional high-resolution simulations will be conducted to generate a comprehensive understanding of the morphological evolution patterns. The outputs will enable quantification of sediment budgets and transport mechanisms throughout the river system.
By establishing quantitative relationships between anthropogenic activities, hydrodynamic conditions, and resultant morphological transformations, this research aims to identify mitigation and conservation strategies to protect this unique environment.

Bussi et al. 2021. Impact of dams and climate change on suspended sediment flux to the Mekong Delta. Science of the Total Environment, 755, p.142468.
Jordan et al. 2019. Sand mining in the Mekong Delta revisited–current scales of local sediment deficits, Sci. Rep., 9, 17823

title: Adapting and Evaluating Digital Interventions for Character Strengths and Resilience in Vietnamese Adolescents
description: This project seeks to address the growing need for culturally sensitive mental health support among Vietnamese adolescents. It will explore the links between early experiences, cultural norms, and the development of key character strengths—including but not limited to courage, empathy, forgiveness, gratitude, hope, humility, patience, and self-efficacy—that are crucial for building resilience, and hence flourishing in the face of external stressors. The aim is to investigate how factors like childhood emotional neglect, cultural attitudes toward emotions and parenting, and differing philosophical conceptions of character shape these strengths and create unique challenges in the Vietnamese context. Building on these understandings, the primary objective is to co-design and pilot a local adaptation of a technology-assisted intervention: i.e., a Vietnamese version of a mobile application or online platform incorporating principles from evidence-based therapies (like Emotion-Focused Therapy and Dialectical Behaviour Therapy) and positive psychology exercises, all tailored to local cultural contexts. The project will use a mixed-methods approach, combining surveys and in-depth interviews with adolescents about their character strengths, to ensure the interventions are both empirically grounded and culturally relevant. The ultimate goal is to create accessible, scalable tools that empower Vietnamese youth to build resilience and flourish in contemporary society.

title: Multi-View Learning for Multi-Omic Gut Microbiome Analysis
description:
Understanding complex biological systems requires integrating diverse datasets that capture complementary aspects of the same biological processes. In gut microbiome research, metagenomic sequencing, metabolomic profiling, and clinical phenotypes each provide valuable but incomplete perspectives on host–microbe interactions. Combining these heterogeneous data sources in a meaningful way remains a major challenge due to substantial differences in structure, scale, and completeness. Existing multi-modal learning methods offer a foundation for heterogeneous data integration, but they struggle with the realities of biological data (such as noise, high dimensionality, small sample sizes, and strong confounding influences from diet, medication, and lifestyle, etc.). Additional challenges include irregular temporal sampling in longitudinal studies, misaligned or partially missing modalities across individuals, limited ground-truth labels for supervised learning, and significant domain shifts across populations.
This proposal aims to develop advanced machine learning models for multi-modal microbiome data integration to address these challenges. The project will explore cross-modal transformers, variational latent-space alignment models, and fusion networks designed to handle compositional and sparse data and robustly learn from incomplete or irregularly sampled modalities. These models will support practical applications in gut microbiome analysis, including identifying microbe–metabolite interactions, mapping host–microbe signaling signatures, and discovering predictive biomarkers of gastrointestinal diseases. This research seeks to advance both the methodological foundations of multi-modal machine learning, and our understanding of how the gut microbiome influences human health.

title: Cybersecurity for Human-Centric Digital Supply Chains: A Holistic Framework for Behavioral, Technological, and Interorganizational Resilience
description: The rapid digitalization of supply chains through Industry 4.0 technologies has enhanced connectivity and automation but simultaneously expanded cyber vulnerabilities across interconnected networks. As cyber threats—especially phishing enabled by human behavioral biases—continue to propagate across multi-tier systems, many firms, particularly SMEs, struggle with misaligned cybersecurity practices, uneven digital readiness, and fragmented governance. Current literature highlights the need for integrated approaches that incorporate human-centric behavioral factors, technological threat modeling, and interorganizational coordination mechanisms to strengthen cyber resilience. This project proposes a holistic framework that links user acceptance theories, CPS–IoT–blockchain threat architectures, risk propagation models, and resilience engineering principles while employing scientometric and text-mining techniques to map research evolution and identify governance and human-factor gaps. The expected contribution is a human-centered model that improves behavioral security, aligns cross-organizational policies, and optimizes cybersecurity investments for more resilient and sustainable digital supply chains.

title: Investor Sentiment, Earnings Surprises, and Market Anomalies Leveraging Generative AI in Capital Market Research
description:
This project aims to integrate behavioral finance with AI-driven sentiment analysis to investigate how investor sentiment influences market reactions to earnings surprises, and whether it amplifies or mitigates anomalies such as post-earnings announcement drift (PEAD).
The relationship between accounting information and capital markets remains a central theme in financial accounting research. A key focus has been how investors interpret and respond to earnings announcements, particularly in the context of PEAD. Recent work highlights investor attention and sentiment as important mechanisms influencing how markets process such information. With the rise of digital platforms, sentiment now spreads rapidly through social media and online news, increasingly shaping trading behavior.
At the same time, advances in large language models (LLMs), such as ChatGPT, offer powerful new tools for extracting sentiment from unstructured online data, complementing traditional proxies like Google Trends or media coverage. This project will employ LLMs to analyze sentiment from digital sources and assess whether these AI-powered measures explain investor overreaction or underreaction to earnings surprises. It will also test whether such sentiment dynamics intensify or dampen anomalies like PEAD.
By bridging behavioral finance with frontier methods in generative AI, this study seeks to advance understanding of how sentiment, attention, and accounting information jointly influence price discovery and asset pricing in modern financial markets.
team_leader: Samuel Buertey, Anh Tuan Nguyen; Ha Xuan Son

title: Strategic Market Learning and Innovation in a Tech-driven Environment
description:
Artificial intelligence (AI), digital platforms, and data-intensive tools are transforming how organizations learn and apply knowledge for innovation. In fast-evolving markets, particularly those marked by short product cycles, customer unpredictability, and continuous technological shifts, firms must practice innovation both incrementally and radically. However, little is known about how organizational learning across multiple levels (individuals, functions, and organization-wide) supports this dual innovation capability.
This project explores how firms develop and leverage market learning under conditions of technology intensity and market turbulence. It focuses on how multi-level learning structures shape a firm’s ability to balance exploitative and exploratory innovation. By taking a dynamic, process-oriented view of how knowledge is generated, shared, and acted upon, the study aims to explain firms’ adaptiveness and sustain innovative performance in tech-driven contexts.
The research adopts a sequential mixed-methods design. In Stage 1, multi-level and curvilinear regression models will be applied to survey data from functional units in Vietnamese enterprises to examine how internal learning behaviors and contextual conditions affect dual innovation. In Stage 2, qualitative case studies will be used to explore how organizations reconfigure routines, digital tools, and cross unit collaboration to enhance learning-based adaptation.
This project contributes to the fields of organizational learning and innovation ambidexterity by clarifying how market-oriented knowledge processes operate in intensive technology settings. It offers strategic insights for firms seeking to strengthen their innovation capabilities through adaptive learning infrastructures and tech-enabled knowledge flows.
team_leader: Phuong Hoang, Professor Robert McClelland; Dr Qui Nguyen

title: Learning Cross-Modal Alignment under Weak Supervision for Multimodal Clinical Prediction
description:
Multimodal learning in biomedicine seeks to integrate heterogeneous data sources - such as radiology (CT/MRI/PET), histopathology whole-slide images (WSI), and clinical data - to improve diagnostic and prognostic accuracy. Recent advances in machine learning, including contrastive multimodal pretraining, cross-attention transformers, and multimodal foundation models, have significantly enhanced predictive performance. However, these approaches largely rely on implicit alignment in latent representation spaces, which often lack interpretability and robustness, especially when modalities are weakly paired, partially missing, or semantically heterogeneous.
This project proposes an approach for learning interpretable and robust cross-modal alignment in clinical prediction. The approach is to learn explicit correspondences across modalities (such as linking imaging regions, histopathological patterns, and clinical data) to improve the integration of complementary diagnostic information. The project will investigate how cross-modal correspondence can enhance predictive accuracy for diagnosis and prognosis while providing clinically meaningful explanations, especially under weak supervision.
Evaluation will focus on predictive performance, robustness across cohorts, and interpretability. This research aims to advance multimodal biomedical AI toward more accurate, robust, and interpretable models, with broad implications for translational medical AI.
References
1. E. Warner et. al., “Multimodal machine learning in image-based and clinical biomedicine: Survey and prospects,” IJCV vol. 132, no. 9, pp. 3753–3769, 2024.
2. J. Lipkova et al., “Artificial intelligence for multimodal data integration in oncology,” Cancer Cell, vol. 40, no. 10, pp. 1095–1110, 2022.
